title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: A government think-tank has commissioned a report on the relationship between income inequality and educational attainment. Your report should identify the main ways in which economic inequality affects educational outcomes, assess the effectiveness of existing policies to address this, and recommend two or three measures that could reduce the educational attainment gap.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on Income Inequality and Educational Attainment**

**Introduction**
This report examines the mechanisms through which income inequality affects educational attainment, evaluates the impact of current policy interventions, and proposes recommendations to reduce the attainment gap. Evidence is drawn from longitudinal studies, PISA data and a review of domestic and international policy literature.

**Impact of Income Inequality on Educational Outcomes**
Economic inequality affects educational attainment through several interconnected mechanisms. Children from lower-income households are more likely to attend under-resourced schools, to lack access to private tutoring and extracurricular activities, and to experience housing instability that disrupts learning. Research by the Education Policy Institute indicates that by the time they sit GCSEs, disadvantaged pupils in England are on average 18 months behind their more affluent peers. Additionally, the cognitive burden of financial stress in households directly impairs children's capacity for sustained academic focus.

**Assessment of Current Policy**
The Pupil Premium has channelled significant additional resources into schools serving disadvantaged communities and has produced measurable, if modest, improvements in attainment in some contexts. However, evidence suggests that its impact has been inconsistent, partly because school autonomy in its deployment has led to wide variation in how effectively it is targeted.

**Recommendations**
Three measures are proposed: strengthening accountability requirements for Pupil Premium spending; expanding universal free school meal provision to all primary-age children; and investing in high-quality early years provision in low-income areas, where the returns to educational investment are highest.

**Conclusion**
Targeted, evidence-based intervention in early childhood and school years offers the most effective pathway to reducing the attainment gap.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt: You have seen an advertisement for a new community initiative in your area that aims to provide free tutoring, mentoring and career guidance to young people from disadvantaged backgrounds. Write an email to the initiative's coordinator expressing your interest in volunteering, explaining what skills and experience you could contribute, and asking about the time commitment and any training provided.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Subject: Volunteer Enquiry — Brightpath Community Initiative

Dear Ms Okonkwo,

I am writing to express my strong interest in volunteering with the Brightpath Community Initiative, having come across your advertisement in the Eastfield Community Newsletter. The work you are doing to support young people from disadvantaged backgrounds aligns closely with values I hold both professionally and personally, and I would welcome the opportunity to contribute.

In terms of the skills and experience I could offer, I have worked for the past seven years as a secondary school English and history teacher, and I have considerable experience supporting students who face barriers to learning, including those with limited access to academic support at home. I am comfortable delivering structured tutoring in both subjects and believe I could contribute effectively to the mentoring strand of the programme as well, drawing on my experience of supporting students through sixth-form and university applications.

Beyond subject-specific support, I have facilitated workshops on study skills and academic writing for students in Years 10 and 11, and would be happy to adapt these materials for use within the Brightpath sessions if that would be useful.

I would be grateful if you could provide some information about the expected time commitment — in particular, whether sessions are typically held during evenings or weekends — and whether volunteers receive any structured training or induction before working with participants.

Thank you for considering my enquiry. I look forward to hearing from you.

With best wishes,
Rachel Oduya

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently read a non-fiction book called 'The Price of Inequality' which explores the causes and consequences of income inequality in modern economies. Write a review for an economics blog, evaluating the book's arguments, assessing the strength of its evidence, and recommending it to readers interested in social policy and economics.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Rigorous, Readable and Urgent: A Review of 'The Price of Inequality'**

'The Price of Inequality' by Dr Celine Marchetti is an ambitious and largely successful attempt to synthesise a vast body of economic research on income inequality into an argument accessible to general readers without sacrificing intellectual substance.

Marchetti's central thesis — that inequality is not merely a distributional problem but a structural drag on economic efficiency, social cohesion and democratic governance — is developed with considerable analytical skill. She moves fluently between macroeconomic data and granular case studies, and her command of the research literature is evident throughout. Particularly compelling is her treatment of the relationship between inequality and health outcomes, where she marshals cross-national comparative data to demonstrate that the negative consequences of economic disparity extend far beyond the lowest income groups.

The book's treatment of policy solutions is equally rigorous. Rather than advocating ideologically for a single approach, Marchetti evaluates the evidence on progressive taxation, universal basic services, collective bargaining rights and investment in early childhood education, assessing each against empirical outcomes rather than political preference. This methodological discipline is one of the book's most valuable qualities.

If the book has a weakness, it is that the later chapters addressing global dimensions of inequality — particularly the interaction between domestic redistribution and international tax avoidance — would benefit from greater depth given their centrality to the argument.

'The Price of Inequality' is highly recommended to anyone interested in economic policy, social justice or the political economy of modern capitalism. It is among the most cogent contributions to this debate in recent years.
